summary: Jeff Atwood reviews Dan Ariely's 'Predictably Irrational' and presents nine psychological manipulation tactics that marketers exploit to influence consumer behavior. The post examines cognitive biases including anchoring, the power of 'free,' loss aversion, and the endowment effect, providing concrete examples from behavioral economics research. For each tactic, Atwood offers practical defensive strategies to help readers recognize and resist manipulation. The core message is that understanding our irrational tendencies is essential self-defense against those who would exploit them.
key_points: Marketers deliberately exploit predictable human irrationality through tactics like false comparisons, anchoring, and leveraging 'free' offers
The endowment effect causes us to dramatically overvalue things we already own - Duke students valued basketball tickets they won at $2,400 while non-winners would only pay $170
Loss aversion is so powerful that MIT students sacrificed 15% of their earnings just to keep worthless options open, even when told the exact payoffs
Price creates expectations that affect perceived quality - subjects reported more pain relief from a fake painkiller priced at $2.50 than the identical pill priced at $0.10
Awareness of these cognitive biases is the first step to defending against manipulation - we don't need to be perfectly rational, just aware of when we're most vulnerable
money_quotes: If you don't understand how these irrational behaviors are triggered, the marketing weasels will use them against you.

You will tend to overestimate the value of items you get for free. Resist this by viewing free stuff skeptically rather than welcoming it with open arms. If it was really that great, why would it be free?
When you use a free service or product, you are implicitly endorsing and encouraging the provider, effectively beating a path to their door.
The value of what you've spent so far on a service, product, or relationship – in effort or money – is probably far less than you think. Be willing to walk away.
We feel compelled to preserve options, even at great expense, even when it doesn't make sense.
Price often has nothing to do with value. Expensive is not synonymous with quality.
Don't fall prey to the 'moneymoon'; just because you paid for something doesn't mean it's automatically worthwhile.

Everyone is irrational sometimes, and that's OK. We're not perfectly logical Vulcans, after all. The trick is training yourself to know when you're most likely to make irrational choices, and to resist those impulses.
key_insight: Understanding the predictable patterns of human irrationality is essential self-defense against marketers who systematically exploit these cognitive biases for profit.
